sources.notes: Read the complete statement and constraints: subarrays may be empty, all but at most one element are -1 or 1, n is at most 200000, and the sum of n over tests is at most 200000.

Read the official editorial section and embedded Python solution. The tutorial prose widget was unavailable, but the code confirms two attainable integer ranges, prefix-extrema handling around the exceptional value, and linear-time merging.

Actually read. It independently proves that a {-1,1} segment attains every sum between its minimum and maximum subarray sums, then derives the exceptional-containing range from left suffix and right prefix extrema.

Re-derived the unit-step no-gaps lemma, the two-range decomposition, and the no-exceptional-element case. Exhaustive comparison with all subarrays matched on 18440 generated arrays of length at most 8.

annotation.summary: Output every distinct sum of a possibly empty contiguous subarray when all array elements except at most one are -1 or 1.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= t <= 10000, 1 <= n <= 200000, sum(n) <= 200000, -1e9 <= a_i <= 1e9, and at most one a_i is outside {-1,1}. The answer has O(n) values despite a possible gap of size about 1e9 between its two ranges.
annotation.core_idea: In any {-1,1} segment, the sums of intervals including the empty interval form every integer from the minimum subarray sum through the maximum subarray sum: shrink an extremal interval to empty one endpoint at a time, and the running sum changes by exactly 1 in absolute value, so it crosses every intermediate integer. If there is no exceptional element, this gives the whole answer. Otherwise split at its position p. Subarrays avoiding p lie wholly on the left or right; both attainable ranges contain 0, so their union is one interval whose endpoints are the smaller minimum and larger maximum subarray sums of the two sides. A subarray containing p consists of a suffix of the left side, a_p, and a prefix of the right side. The suffix-sum and prefix-sum walks also move by +/-1, so each fills an integer interval; their sums with a_p fill a second interval. Merge or concatenate the two intervals and enumerate them in sorted order.
annotation.complexity: O(n + K) time and O(K) space when the K output values are materialized; K = O(n). The extrema themselves need O(1) working memory.
tricks: Include the empty subarray so every ordinary-side range contains 0.
Use the unit-step no-gaps lemma to replace many subarrays by interval endpoints.
Split only at the unique exceptional value; every subarray either avoids it or contains it.
For containing subarrays, combine a left suffix-sum interval and a right prefix-sum interval.
Handle the case with no exceptional value as one ordinary {-1,1} segment.

solution_variants.name: split-at-exceptional-value
solution_variants.summary: Find the exceptional position if it exists. Compute minimum and maximum subarray sums on each {-1,1} side, plus minimum and maximum suffix sums on the left and prefix sums on the right. These values define at most two integer intervals; merge overlaps or adjacency and output their union.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n + K) time and O(K) output storage, with K = O(n)

tags.evidence: Because every relevant prefix, suffix, or endpoint-shrinking sum walk changes by exactly +/-1, every integer between its minimum and maximum is attained; this no-gaps fact is what compresses all subarray sums into at most two ranges.
tags.description: Prove that an attainable set of integer values has no gaps, usually because a process moves in unit steps, and represent the entire set by its minimum and maximum. Use when the no-gaps proof is essential, not merely when answers happen to be bounded by an interval.
tags.created_reason: The existing algorithm/interval-union tag covers merging explicit intervals and trick/interval-feasibility covers intersecting bounds; neither captures the reusable proof that a discrete reachable-value set itself is contiguous, common in +/-1 walks and unit-change processes.

tags.evidence: The endpoints for sums avoiding the exceptional value are the minimum and maximum subarray sums of the two ordinary sides, computed by Kadane-style extrema scans.
The exact range of sums containing the exceptional element is determined by the minimum and maximum suffix sums to its left and prefix sums to its right.
The final distinct answer is represented and emitted as the sorted union of at most two integer intervals, merging them when they overlap or are adjacent.
